title: Bank Statement Analysis for Food Trucks: Getting a Business Loan
seo_title: Bank Statement Analysis for Food Trucks
meta: Food trucks have mobile, event-driven revenue that looks irregular on bank statements. Learn how lenders analyze food truck financials and what loan options are available.
content:
Food Truck Cash Flow: What Lenders See

Food truck businesses are cash-intensive, mobile, and highly variable — which makes bank statement analysis both important and nuanced. Revenue depends on location, weather, events, and season in ways that fixed restaurant locations don't.

How Food Truck Revenue Appears on Statements

  • Square / Stripe / PayPal settlements: Daily deposits following service days
  • Cash deposits: Bundled deposits from cash-heavy events
  • Catering contract payments: Larger deposits for private events or corporate catering
  • Commissary fees: Regular outflows for commercial kitchen rental
  • Location permit fees: Periodic payments for food truck park spaces

A key pattern: active days show deposits, non-operating days show nothing. A food truck that operates 4 days/week will show deposits 4 times per week, not daily like a brick-and-mortar restaurant.

What Makes Food Truck Statements Tricky

The event-driven, seasonal nature of food truck revenue creates patterns that concern lenders unfamiliar with the business:

  • Winter months may show near-zero deposits (outdoor events stop)
  • Festival season creates large, irregular deposits
  • Revenue can drop sharply if a regular location is lost
  • Equipment breakdowns create sudden revenue gaps

AI tools like StatementScrub analyze 12-month deposit patterns and calculate consistency scores — which helps lenders understand what's seasonal variability vs. genuine business deterioration.

Loan Options for Food Trucks

  • Equipment financing: For truck purchase, refurbishment, or kitchen equipment
  • MCA / short-term business loans: Fast access to working capital; common in the food service industry
  • SBA microloans: For smaller amounts ($50K or less); lower rates than MCAs
  • CDFI loans: Community development lenders often serve food entrepreneurs

Related: Restaurant bank statement analysis | Seasonal income bank statement analysis

Bottom Line

Food truck bank statements require seasonal context and understanding of event-driven revenue. Twelve months is the minimum useful window for analysis. Lenders who apply the right context will find food trucks to be straightforward — those who don't will see irregularity where there's actually a functioning seasonal business model.
